In this view you can validate all of the paths that were accessed in your application, regardless of when they were accessed through the lifetime of a user session.
The conversion rate is the percentage of users who completed the conversion goal in this specific path.
Entry paths are the paths a user session started, i.e. the first path they saw when they opened your website.
The conversion rate is the percentage of users who completed the conversion goal after the first path in their session being this path.
End paths are the last path a user visited before their session ended, i.e. the last path they saw before leaving your website/closing the browser/turning their computer off.
You'll be able to verify when someone leaves your website by clicking an outbound link (to a separate domain)
Channels are the different sources that bring traffic to your website, e.g. Paid Search, Organic Social, Direct, etc.
Retention creates a cohort of unique users who performed any event for the first time in the last week. It then tracks the percentage of users who return to perform any event in the following weeks.

You want the numbers to be the highest possible, suggesting that people that come to your page continue coming to your page - and performing an actions. Also, the further down the table the higher the numbers should be (or at least as high), which would indicate that you're either increasing or keeping your retention at the same level.
Active hours displays a heatmap showing the number of unique users who performed any pageview event, broken down by hour of the day and day of the week.
Active hours displays a heatmap showing the total number of pageviews, broken down by hour of the day and day of the week.
Error tracking allows you to track, investigate, and resolve exceptions your customers face.
See which pages are causing frustration by monitoring rage clicks, dead clicks, and errors.
A dead click is a click that doesn't result in any action. E.g. an image that looks like a button.
Rageclicks are collected when a user clicks on a static element more than three times in a one-second window.
Errors are JavaScript exceptions that occur when users interact with your site.
These are captured automatically and can help identify broken functionality, failed API calls, or other technical issues that frustrate users.

Some languages, bundlers and frameworks obfuscate code making debugging difficult. Source maps are essential to transform code back to the original source. Choose the option that matches your setup to ensure stack traces are readable.

Once you've built your application and have bundled assets that serve your site, inject the context required by PostHog to associate the maps with the served code. You will then need to upload the modified assets to PostHog. Both of these operations can be done by running the respective sourcemap commands.

Not all languages and frameworks are minified. For example Python and Node code often remains readable and there is no need to upload source maps.
If source maps are publicly available PostHog can fetch and provide readable stack traces without any additional setup.
Should you wish to keep your source maps private you will need to upload source maps during your build process to see unminified code in your stack traces.

Different PostHog models have semi-structured data fields in them, like "events.properties". We can export these fields to Redshift as a "SUPER" type column, or a "VARCHAR" column. We recommend "SUPER" over "VARCHAR" as "VARCHAR" has a strict length limit that applies on the entire document, whereas with "SUPER" the limit applies on each value in the document.
Choose the SQL command used by the batch export. "COPY" has the best performance but requires an S3 bucket we can connect to. "INSERT" performs worse but without any additional requirements.

In order to execute a COPY SQL command, the batch export will first export files to an S3 bucket, which requires credentials to access it. After the files are available in the bucket, we will instruct Redshift to copy them into the configured table. As part of this, the batch export needs to specify to Redshift how to access your bucket. For this reason, additionally to the first set of credentials used by the batch export, either an IAM role (recommended), or the same or a different set of credentials is required.
Redshift needs to authenticate to COPY data from your S3 bucket. Choose whether to assume an IAM role or to provide it with credentials to access the S3 bucket.

Configure how conversions are attributed to marketing campaigns.

The attribution window determines how far back in time to look for marketing touchpoints when attributing conversions. Example: With a 30-day window, if someone converts today, we'll look back 30 days for any UTM campaigns they interacted with. Recommendation: Use 30-60 days for short sales cycles, 90+ days for longer consideration periods.

Attribution mode determines how credit for a conversion is distributed across marketing touchpoints. First touch: 100% credit to the first touchpoint. Last touch: 100% credit to the last touchpoint. Linear: Equal credit across all touchpoints. Time decay: More credit to recent touchpoints, with credit halving at regular intervals. Position based: 40% first, 40% last, 20% distributed among middle touchpoints.

When breaking down funnels, it's possible that the same properties don't exist on every event. For example, if you want to break down by browser on a funnel that contains both frontend and backend events.
In this case, you can choose from which step the properties should be selected from by modifying the attribution type. There are four modes to choose from:
First touchpoint: the first property value seen in any of the steps is chosen.
Last touchpoint: the last property value seen from all steps is chosen.
All steps: the property value must be seen in all steps to be considered in the funnel.
Specific step: only the property value seen at the selected step is chosen.

A CSP is an instruction to the browser on what assets are allowed to be loaded and what domains your site can send information to. It's a very powerful security mechanism, that can be super tricky to configure.

CSP Reporting lets you track your CSP by sending reports to PostHog when a CSP violation occurs. This helps you see when CSP misconfiguration, web site changes, or security flaws are causing problems.
See our docs for some examples.
Set this URL for both the report-to and report-uri endpoints
version: the version for the current CSP. This helps you track impact of changes to your CSP.
session_id: the PostHog UUIDv7 session id. Helps you link CSP violations to session replay.
distinct_id: the distinct id for the current user. So you can track which users are being affected
sample_rate: the sample rate for the current CSP. Lets you control the volume of reports you ingest.
